era_context: The late 15th century continues with European nation-states consolidating power, the Ottoman Empire expanding, and the Ming Dynasty maintaining centralised rule in China. The Italian Renaissance is in full flourish. The Hundred Years' War between England and France has recently concluded (1453), reshaping Western European politics. In Africa, the Portuguese are establishing trading posts along the coast.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Indigenous Americas (no written records in European tradition)

events.title: Thirteen Years' War continues: Polish-Teutonic Order conflict
events.description: The Thirteen Years' War between the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and the Teutonic Order was ongoing in 1465, with no resolution yet in sight. The conflict had begun in 1454 and would continue until 1466. In 1465, both sides were engaged in military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations. The war was draining resources from both powers and would ultimately result in a Polish victory.
key_figures: Ludwig von Vietinghoff (Grand Master of the Teutonic Order)
events.certainty_note: The Thirteen Years' War is well-documented; 1465 falls within the confirmed period of conflict.

events.title: Mehmed II campaigns in the Balkans; Ottoman expansion continues
events.description: Mehmed II, the Conqueror, continued his military campaigns to consolidate Ottoman control over the Balkans in 1465. Following the conquest of Constantinople in 1453, Mehmed was systematically subjugating or eliminating rival powers in the region. His campaigns in 1465 were part of a broader strategy to establish Ottoman hegemony over the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans.
events.certainty_note: Mehmed II's military activities are well-documented in Ottoman and Venetian sources.

events.title: Chenghua Emperor's reign in Ming China; eunuch influence grows
events.description: The Chenghua Emperor (Zhu Jianshen) had recently ascended to the Ming throne in 1464 and was consolidating his power in 1465. His reign was marked by the growing influence of eunuchs in court politics, particularly the Wang Zhen faction. This period saw the continuation of Ming administrative structures and the maintenance of the tributary system in East Asia, though the increasing power of eunuchs would later become a source of instability.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Ming court records are extensive and contemporary.
cross_references: Part of Ming Dynasty political history (1368–1644)

events.title: Italian Renaissance: Cosimo de' Medici's influence in Florence
events.description: In 1465, Florence was under the de facto control of Cosimo de' Medici (Cosimo the Elder), who had established the Medici family as the dominant power in the city. Cosimo was a major patron of the arts and learning, supporting artists, architects, and scholars. His influence was shaping Florence into a centre of Renaissance culture and innovation. In 1465, Cosimo was in his late 80s and would die in 1464 (note: this entry contains a chronological issue; see disconfirming evidence).

sources.name: Giorgio Vasari, Lives of the Artists

events.certainty_note: Cosimo de' Medici's death in 1464 is well-documented; in 1465, his son Piero de' Medici had assumed leadership of the family.
cross_references: Part of Medici family dominance in Florence (1434–1494)

events.title: Portuguese exploration: continued expansion along African coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers, under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, continued their systematic exploration of the African coast in 1465. Portuguese trading posts were being established along the Guinea coast, and explorers were pushing further south. These expeditions were laying the groundwork for the later Age of Exploration and establishing Portuguese commercial dominance in West African trade.

sources.name: Portuguese royal archives and navigation records

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of this period is documented, though specific dates for individual voyages can vary.
cross_references: Part of Portuguese Age of Exploration (1415–1500s)

disconfirming_evidence: Cosimo de' Medici died in 1464, not 1465. The entry above incorrectly suggests his influence was continuing in 1465 as if he were still alive. In 1465, his son Piero de' Medici (Piero the Gouty) had assumed leadership of the Medici family and Florence. This is a correction to the historical record. Additionally, the characterisation of Cosimo's death as occurring in 1464 is confirmed, making the 1465 entry anachronistic.
historiographic_note: The year 1465 is well-documented in European sources, particularly for military and political events. Italian Renaissance sources are particularly rich. However, as with 1466, non-European regions are underrepresented. The sources available are often written from the perspective of European powers or East Asian courts. The historical record for 1465 is heavily weighted toward European events, with limited documentation of events in Africa, the Americas, and Central Asia.

graph_edges.note: The Thirteen Years' War continued in 1465 and concluded in 1466
Mehmed II's campaigns continued across both years
Italian Renaissance cultural flourishing across both years
